generations.text:
My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea, as ever, is restless, and the wind howls like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lighthouse. Tonight, a storm is upon us—one that may not spare the land, nor the soul. I do not know if I shall see another dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not be the man I once was.

Five years have passed since you left, and though the days have dragged on, I have not forgotten the last words we exchanged. You called me a fool, a man who clung to duty like a drowning man to driftwood. I did not answer you. I could not. For what would words have done? They would have been but echoes in the void you left behind.

I have kept this lighthouse, as I have kept my word, though I fear the sea has taken more from me than I can ever repay. The light still burns, though I sometimes wonder if it is merely a beacon for the ghosts of those who have perished in the dark. I have seen many ships lost to the tempest, and I have watched their crewmen's faces as they vanished into the mist, their cries swallowed by the waves. I have come to know the sea as a cruel and indifferent mistress, and I have learned to fear her more than I once did.

I do not write to beg for your

My dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. The sea howls like a beast in pain, and the wind whips through the cracks of this old lighthouse as if it too wishes to be heard. Tonight, the storm comes with a fury I have never known, and I fear the sea may claim me before dawn. But I must write, for if I do not, I will be left with the silence of my regrets, and you will never know the truth of what I have felt.

Five years have passed since the last time our eyes met, and in that time, the world has changed, yet I have not. You have grown into a woman of strength and grace, and I have remained a man of shadows and stone. I have watched the waves, day after day, and I have prayed for the day I might see your face again. But time has been a cruel master, and I have let the years slip through my fingers like the sand of the hourglass.

I know you have heard of my folly. I know you have heard the whispers of the town, the tales of a man who abandoned his family for the sea. But I beg you, my dear Eliza, do not believe them. I was not a man who chose to leave you. I was a man who chose to protect you. The sea is a fickle mistress, and I feared that if I stayed, I

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The sea has been restless all day, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I fear it may be my last night on this earth, and I must speak now, before silence swallows my voice forever.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I have not heard from you, nor have I dared to write, for fear that my words might be as sharp as the rocks that jut from these cliffs. Yet now, as the storm gathers strength and the waves threaten to devour the lighthouse, I find myself compelled to reach out—though I know you may not wish to hear from me.

I have been alone for so long, my days spent in the glow of the beacon, my nights in the company of the sea. The lighthouse has become my only companion, and the wind has whispered stories of you, of your laughter, of your dreams, to me as I stood watch. I have longed for your return, though I never dared to hope.

I know you have chosen your own path, and I have chosen mine. I have tried to forgive you, though the sting of your absence lingers like the salt on my skin. I have tried to forget, though the memory of your last words—cold, sharp, and final—still lingers in my mind like

**April 15th, 1892**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars in fury, and I can feel the very foundations of this place trembling beneath me. I’ve kept the light burning all night, though I know it may not be enough. The storm is coming, and I’m not sure I’ll survive it.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, bitter years since you left. I’ve tried to forget, to bury the memories in the salt air and the endless nights. But tonight, the storm brings with it a different kind of wind—one that stirs the ghosts of the past, and I can’t help but think of you.

I remember the last time we spoke. You were angry, I was tired. You said I’d never understand what you were going through. You said I’d never see the world as you did. And I believed you. I told you I was sorry, that I didn’t mean to fail you. But I didn’t know how to fix what I’d broken. I didn’t know how to be the father you needed.

You left that night, not with words, but with silence. I’ve spent every day since trying to find a way to reach you again. I’ve sent letters, but they’ve gone unanswered. I’ve written to your

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has grown heavier with each passing year. The night air is thick with the scent of salt and the promise of tempest, and I find myself staring into the flickering glow of the beacon, wondering if it will be my last. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves, in their fury, seem to mock the very notion of peace.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, I have come to understand the weight of silence. I have tried, Eliza, I have tried to reach you, to send letters that were never answered, to write words that were never read. I have told myself that you were merely distant, that the sea had taken your heart, as it had taken mine. But I now know the truth—your silence was not of indifference, but of sorrow. And I have borne the burden of that sorrow alone.

You were a child when I last saw you, and I have spent these years watching the world change, while you have grown into a woman I no longer recognize. I see your face in the faces of the sailors who come to seek shelter, in the haggard expressions of those who have lost their way. I hear your voice in the cries of the wind, in the creak of the lighthouse, in the distant wail of the sea. And yet, I have not seen you, nor

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It’s been five years since you left, and five years since I’ve had the strength to write your name without my hands trembling. The sea has been restless these past few days, and I fear the storm that’s gathering on the horizon may be more than just a squall. I’ve been watching the sky all evening, and it’s dark as a grave, the clouds swirling like a living thing, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but tonight, I feel the weight of the world pressing down on me more than ever.

I’ve tried to keep the lighthouse running, to keep the light burning for those who sail these waters, but I’m not sure I can do it much longer. My back aches from the cold, my hands are numb, and the lantern’s gears are getting slower. I’ve been thinking about the last time we spoke, when you stood at the door and said you were leaving. You said you needed to find your own way, and I let you go. I told myself it was for the best. But now, as I sit here in this cold, lonely tower, I wonder if I made the biggest mistake of my life.

You were always so full of fire, Eliza. That’s what I loved about you—your spirit, your courage. But you never stopped to think about the ones who stayed behind. I’ve always

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the arms of some fine gentleman, or perhaps in the arms of a new husband, as I once was. I write with a trembling hand, for the wind howls like the devil himself, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, not since the day you left this lighthouse, and I have not spoken to anyone else since. But tonight, the storm is coming, and I do not believe I will see the dawn.

I have always been a man of quiet words, Eleanor. You know this. I have never been one for grand speeches or flowery sentiments. I have spent my life watching the sea, listening to its moods, and knowing when it is angry. And now, I feel it is angry with me. The wind is rising, the waves are rising, and I fear I may not be able to keep the light burning through the night. If the light goes out, the ships will crash, and the sea will claim more lives than it has ever known.

But I do not write this to beg your forgiveness. I write this because I have something to say before I may be gone.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. I know you have grown into a woman of strength and spirit, and I have no doubt you have made your own way in the world

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not be the same as hearing your voice or feeling your embrace. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have often found myself wondering what path you have taken in the world, what joys or sorrows you may have encountered. I have tried to keep myself occupied with the duties of this lighthouse, but the silence has grown heavy of late.

Tonight, a storm is coming—one that threatens to tear the very foundations of this cliff apart. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, in its fury, has begun to lash against the rocks with a vengeance. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I can only hope that its light will guide the way for those who may be lost in the darkness.

I have not spoken to you in so long, and yet I find myself compelled to write. There are things I have not said, things that have weighed on my heart like the heavy stones of this lighthouse. I have always been a man of few words, but now I find that my silence has become a prison of its own. I have watched the sea for years, and I have learned to listen to its whispers. Tonight, I hear it speak of endings and beginnings, of storms that must pass and the calm that follows.

I do not know if I will survive this night, Eleanor. The sea is a cruel

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee outside these old stone walls, and the sea roiling as if it too knows the weight of what I must say. The lantern above me flickers, as if it too is weeping, and I can't help but think of you, my dear Eliza, and the years that have passed like a storm that never clears.

Five long years since you left this house, that day you walked out with your head held high and your heart heavy with what I can only guess were your own burdens. I tried to reach you, but you would not listen. You said I was blind to the truth, that I clung to the past like a man who had forgotten how to live. I did not know then, and I still do not know, what truth you saw in that stormy sea, but I have spent every day since wondering if I had been the one to break you, or if you had broken yourself.

You were my light, Eliza. Not the lantern that guides the ships, but the warmth in my heart, the reason I kept going when the days were long and the nights were colder than the wind. I used to tell you stories of the sea, of the stars, of the constellations that never change, even when the world does. You used to listen, and now I wonder if I was just trying to fill the silence

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters of this old lighthouse, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth beneath us. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, with the storm brewing on the horizon, I feel the weight of that silence more than ever.

I know you will not read this, not in the way I would wish. You have turned your back on this house, on this life, on this father. You have never answered my letters, and I have not dared to send another. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves grow fiercer, I feel the need to speak, to be heard, even if it is only by the wind and the sea.

I have spent my days here, alone, watching the horizon, keeping the light burning for those who sail these waters. It is a duty I have taken upon myself, and one I have fulfilled with unwavering care. But I have also been alone, Eleanor. Truly alone. I have not seen you since the day you left, and I have not known how to reach you. I have tried to believe that time would mend what was broken, but it has only deepened the wound.

I have thought of you often, in the quiet hours, when the sea is still and the wind

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the days have passed like the tides, they have not washed away the sorrow of our silence. I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you as distant and cold as the sea that surrounds this lighthouse.

The wind howls against the stone walls as I write, and the waves crash with a fury that speaks of the tempest that is to come. I have seen such storms before, but never have I felt the weight of this one as I do now. The sky is black as ink, and the clouds churn like a beast in torment. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for every storm, and I pray that the light may guide the ships that dare to brave these waters. But I wonder if it will be enough to save me.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be prepared to give his life for the safety of others. It is a duty, a sacred trust, and one I have carried with pride. But tonight, as I look out over the dark sea, I find myself wondering if I have been too proud, if I have been too blind to the truth that has lain before me all these years.

Eleanor, I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear the winds of time and distance have left me with little hope of your reply. Five long years have passed since I last set pen to paper, and I confess I have not dared to write, for fear that my words might bring more sorrow than solace. Yet, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, and the sea roars like a beast in torment, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my daughter, my heart, my only solace in this lonely existence.

You were but a child when you left, and I, in my foolish pride, allowed you to go. I believed that the world would be kinder to you than it was to me. I believed that the sea, though cruel, would grant you a better life than the one I could offer. But I was wrong. I was wrong to let you go, and I am wrong to have let the years pass without a word.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and though the light burns bright, it is not the same as the light of your smile, the warmth of your laughter, the comfort of your presence. Each night, as I stand at the helm and watch the waves crash against the rocks, I am reminded of the darkness that surrounds me, and how I have been blind to the light that was always within reach.

Tonight, the storm is upon us. The wind

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you this evening, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury that I have never known. The waves crash against the cliffs with such violence that I fear the very stones will be torn from the earth. And yet, in this moment of chaos, I find myself thinking of you—of the last time we spoke, of the silence that followed, of the unspoken words that have gathered like mist upon the sea.

Five years have passed since we last exchanged a letter, and I have not written to you since. I have not known what words could befit a father to a daughter who has turned her back on us both. You were once the light in my life, Eleanor, and now it seems that the light has faded, leaving only shadow and sorrow in its place.

I have spent these years watching the beacon from this tower, its glow a beacon not only for the sailors who pass by but for the memories that cling to the walls of this old place. I have thought of you often, and I have wondered if you have ever looked up at the lighthouse from the shore, as I do every night, hoping that perhaps, in some way, we might still be connected.

Tonight, however, the storm is unlike any I have seen. The sky is dark as ink, and the wind carries with it the sound of something ancient and terrible. I have lived through many storms, but none have

My Dearest Clara,

I write to you now, with trembling hands and a heart heavy with the weight of years. It has been five long years since you left our home, and though the days have passed, the ache of your absence remains ever present in my soul. I have spent these years in the solitude of this lighthouse, where the sea howls and the wind sings tales of old, and yet, I have never felt so alone as I do when the moon is high and the night is still.

Tonight, a terrible storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones, a storm that will test the very strength of this old tower and the resolve of a man who has long since lost his way. The sea is restless, the waves rise higher, and the sky is dark with clouds that seem to weep. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night for as long as I can remember, but tonight, I fear it may not be enough to guide those who sail these waters.

I have thought of you often, Clara. I have remembered the laughter that once filled our home, the way your eyes would brighten at the sight of the sea, and the way you would run to the shore to chase the waves. I have missed you more than words can say, and I have prayed for your return. But I know you are far away, and I wonder if you still remember the man who once held you in his arms and promised to

**April 12, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. It has been five long, bitter years since we last spoke, and yet tonight, as the storm howls across the cliffs and the sea roars like a beast in pain, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in all that time.

You were once my greatest pride, my light in the darkness of this lonely life. I remember the day you first came to live with me, a little girl with eyes like the sea and a spirit that burned brighter than the lighthouse itself. You used to sit by the window, watching the waves, and I would tell you stories of ships that had sailed far and wide, of distant lands and distant dreams. You dreamed of the world beyond these rocks, of a life filled with color and laughter, while I, bound by duty and the weight of this stone fortress, could only offer you the quiet comfort of the sea.

But time, as it always does, has a cruel way of changing things. You left, Eleanor, not with a farewell, but with a silence that echoed through the halls of this house like the sound of a bell in the night. I tried to understand, to forgive, to reach out—but you never answered. I feared that my love, though deep, was not enough, and so I let the silence grow between us.

Now

**My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with the ink still wet and the wind howling like a beast at the door. The sea is restless tonight, churning with a fury I’ve never seen before. The lantern’s light flickers, and I’ve been watching it for hours, hoping it might steady me. But the storm is coming, and I fear it may not be the sea that takes me.

Five years. Five years since you left. Five years since I last saw your face. I’ve tried to forget, but the memories are like the tide—always returning, always stronger. I remember the last time we spoke. You were angry, and I was tired. You told me I was a fool to stay here, to waste my life on this rock and this light. You said I’d never be anything more than a man who watches the sea and prays for the ships to come and go.

I didn’t argue. I didn’t even look at you when you walked out. I just stood there, holding the lantern, and let the silence fall between us like a shroud.

But now, as the storm gathers, I find myself wondering if I was wrong. If I was too afraid to leave, too afraid to chase the life you had. I’ve spent my days in this lighthouse, alone, with the sea as my only companion. I’ve seen the ships come and go, the stars blink above me, and the dawn rise like

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1876**

I sit here by the great iron lamp, its glow flickering like a dying ember in the dark. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, that old and ever-hungry beast, roars with a fury I have not known since the day you left. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself writing to you—though I fear my words may be lost in the storm, as I am.

You were a child when you last saw me, a girl with eyes like the sea at dawn, full of wonder and fire. I told you then that I would always be your father, and I meant it. But time, like the tide, has worn away the shores of our bond. You left with your mother, and I have not heard from you since. I do not know if you still live, or if you have moved on to some new life, far from this lonely rock and the sea that has been my companion for thirty years.

I have not written to you because I feared what might be said. You were not the kind of child who took kindly to being told what to do, and I have always been a man of firm hand and steady heart. I thought, perhaps, that if I kept silent, you would come back to me. But you did not

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this by the light of the flickering lamp, its glow trembling like my own hands. The sea howls outside, and the wind sings a mournful tune through the cracks in the stone. I've spent most of the evening listening to it, trying to remember the sound of your voice. It's been five long years since I've seen you, and I fear this may be the last night I'll ever see the dawn.

I’ve been meaning to write to you, but the words always seemed to fall short. The sea has a way of silencing the heart, and I’ve let it do so for too long. I’ve blamed the storm, the loneliness, the burden of this lonely post. But I know now that I've been running from the truth—my own guilt.

I never meant to let you go. I never meant to let the sea take my family from me. I was so consumed by duty, by the responsibility of keeping this lighthouse lit, that I forgot the most important light of all—yours.

I remember the day you left. You were just a girl, no older than sixteen, with your father’s stubbornness and your mother’s grace. You were so angry, Eleanor. I could see it in your eyes. You thought I’d chosen the lighthouse over you. But I didn’t. I chose to keep the light burning so that others might find their way home. I chose

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the sea before a storm. Five years have passed since you left this place, and I've been a prisoner to the waves and the silence of my own thoughts. The lighthouse stands as it always has, a sentinel against the dark, but the light within it has long since dimmed—both in fire and in spirit.

You were a child when you left, no older than ten, and I was a fool to let you go. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world beyond this rock was better than the life I offered you. But I was wrong. I was wrong in every way, and I have paid the price in silence and sorrow.

The storm is coming, Eliza. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea is restless, churning with a fury that has not been seen in decades. I have lit the lamp, as I have done every night since the day you left, but tonight... tonight I fear the light may not be enough. I fear the sea may take me before the morning comes.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if you will even read these words. But if you do, know this: I have not forgotten you. I have not stopped watching over this place, nor have I stopped watching for you. I have not stopped loving you, though I have

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds me in a state of considerable unrest. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside these walls, and the sea, that old, unrelenting foe, roars with a fury I’ve not seen in decades. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, though I fear tonight’s storm may test the very limits of its endurance—and mine.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five years since you slammed the door behind you, leaving me with the weight of silence and the echo of your voice. I’ve tried to write, but the words always seem to falter, like the light that flickers in the storm. I’ve tried to call, but the sea’s voice drowns out any attempt to reach you. And I’ve tried to forget, but the nights are long, and the memories are sharp as the sea’s teeth.

I’ve always believed in the light, you know. Not just the lamp that guides the ships, but the light of purpose, of duty, of love. I’ve spent my life ensuring that no soul is lost to the dark, even if it means sacrificing my own comfort. But tonight, I find myself wondering if I’ve been wrong to keep this light burning for so long. If I’ve been too afraid to let it go

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls like a beast outside, and the sea roars as though it too knows the weight of my sorrow. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn. But if I do, I must tell you this—*I am sorry*.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years of watching the waves crash against these rocks, of listening to the wind whisper your name, and of wondering if you ever thought of me at all. I know the world has moved on, and I should have moved with it. But I have not. I have stayed here, in this lonely tower, because it is all I have left of you.

I’ve always told myself that you were better off without me. That you had your own life, your own dreams, your own future. And perhaps you did. But I have never stopped wondering if you ever thought of me, if you ever missed me, or if you ever truly believed I was the father you once loved.

I know I failed you, Eliza. I know I was too stubborn, too proud, too blind to see the pain I caused. I let pride and anger cloud my judgment, and in doing so, I lost you. I lost the daughter who once ran through the fields with me, who used to laugh as I told

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same state of cold indifference as you have left me. Five years is a long time, and I have long since ceased counting the days. You have not written, nor have you sent word of your life, your children, or even your husband. I have heard nothing but the wind and the sea, and the silence of your absence.

I write to you now not out of spite or longing, but because the storm is coming—great and terrible, as if the heavens themselves are in mourning. The skies have been dark for days, and the sea grows restless. The waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that is unnatural, and the wind howls like a beast in pain. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though I fear it may not be enough to guide the ships through this maelstrom. I have spent the last hour securing the windows, fastening the shutters, and ensuring that the beacon remains unshaken. But I am not so sure it will be enough.

You were always a stubborn child, Eleanor. I remember the day you left, the way you turned your back on me with such finality, as if I had done something to deserve it. I told you I was a lighthouse keeper, not a father. I told you that this life was not for you, that it was not a life

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these ancient walls. The sea has been restless all day, and now the sky is a mass of dark, churning clouds, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though I fear the light may not be enough to guide those who sail these treacherous waters tonight.

It is strange, this loneliness. I have lived here for thirty years, and yet, I have never felt more alone than I do now. The lighthouse is a place of solitude, but it is not the same as the silence of a heart unspoken to. I have not heard from you in five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years. I have tried to write, but the letters always seem to vanish, or are returned with no explanation. I do not know why you have turned your back on me. I thought we had something more than mere blood between us.

I remember the day you left. It was a quiet morning, the sea calm, the sky a pale blue. You stood at the door, your face pale, your eyes full of sorrow. You said you could not stay, that you had to go. I did not argue. I did not try to hold you. I simply nodded, and you walked away. I have not seen you since.

I have tried to believe that you would return, that

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I daresay I have no right to hope for such things. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and I have not dared to write, fearing the weight of my words might be too much for you to bear. Yet now, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon and the winds howl like the wails of the damned, I find myself compelled to reach out once more.

You were but a child when you left, and though I have since grown old and gray, I still remember the way your eyes sparkled with the fire of youth, and the way your laughter could light up even the darkest of nights. I have often wondered if that laughter still echoes in your heart, or if the years have turned it to silence.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza, and I do not know if you will even read these words. But I must try, for I have no one else to confide in. The lighthouse has been my home for many years, a steadfast sentinel against the sea’s wrath. It has seen many storms, but never one like the one that approaches now. The sky is dark as ink, and the wind has risen to a fury that shakes the very stones beneath my feet.

I do not know if I shall survive the night, and if I do, I do not know what

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and I have not spoken your name aloud since. Yet tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars in its wrath, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, and to the memories that cling to me like the salt upon my skin.

You were never the daughter I wished for, nor the one I imagined in my dreams. I raised you in the light of reason, in the cold precision of duty, and in the unyielding certainty of the sea. But you, my child, were never meant for the lighthouse. You were meant for the world beyond its stone walls, for the warmth of hearth and home, for the embrace of a mother’s arms. And yet, you chose to walk away, leaving behind the only home you ever knew.

I have not slept in days, not since the storm began to gather. The waves have grown wilder, the sky darker, and the wind has taken on a voice like the one I once heard in your childhood—full of promise, and then of sorrow. I fear that this night may be the last one I see. The light has been dimming, and the old lantern, though steadfast, is not immune to the ravages of time. I have been told that the storm may last through the night

My Dearest Eliza,

I sit by the great window of this lighthouse, the wind howling like a beast of the night, and I feel the weight of the world upon my shoulders. It has been five long years since I last saw your face, and though the sea may be vast, it cannot compare to the chasm that has opened between us. I write this now, as the storm gathers strength and the waves grow wild, for I do not know if I shall live to see another dawn.
